coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of the carbon-optimized integrated cobalt-based electrode for high cycle performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhixiang
Cai, Wangfeng
Wang, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Co3O4 and nickel–cobalt layered double hydroxide (Ni-Co LDH) have been extensively studied as a typical cobalt-based pseudocapacitive materials. To further improve their electrochemical performance, the cobalt-based materials and carbon were integrated to prepare hierarchical Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays through the hydrothermal and calcination methods. According to the characterization, the results show the Co3O4 nanocones with single-crystalline structure serve as the stable frameworks and the amorphous carbon coated on the Co3O4 nanocones improves the conductivity and the structural stability significantly. Moreover, numerous active sites of Redox reactions, provided by the Ni-Co LDH, improve specific capacitance of electrode. Based on performance measurements, it was illustrated that the integrated Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes possessed the outstanding performance of specific capacitance (1755 F/g at 1 A/g) with remarkable cycling stability (specific capacitance remained at 90.47% after 5000 cycles at the current density of 10 A/g) because of the synergetic effects of high activity and conductivity Co3O4 @C scaffolds and well-defined Ni-Co LDH nanoneedles. The asymmetric supercapacitors, based on Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH // active carbon, showed an extraordinary cyclic stability with the retention rate of specific capacitance is 94.47% after 16000 cycles. Furthermore, prepared devices showed the excellent energy density with 33.02 Wh/kg with the power density was 398.85 W/kg. The excellent electrochemical properties of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes indicate that the multi-component integrated materials have great theoretical significance for the study of high-performance electrode materials, and also reflect that the Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes exhibit outstanding value and extremely promising prospect in practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
Co3O4@C@Ni-Co LDH
Integration electrode
Carbon

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays.
Typical SEM images of active materials at different magnifications. (a, d) Co3O4; (b, e) Co3O4 @C; and (c, f) Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH.
Low-magnification and high-magnification TEM images of (a, d) Co3O4 nanowire, (b, e) Co3O4 @C, and (c, f) Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH.
XRD patterns of the Co3O4; Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH; and Ni-Co LDH.
XPS survey spectra of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH. (a) XPS wide-scan spectra; and (b−e) high-resolution XPS spectra for O, C, Co, and Ni.

CV curves (a) at different scan rates and CP curves (b) at different discharge current densities of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH. (c) specific capacitance at different current densities from 1 to 16 A/g. (d) Impedance Nyquist plots of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH at an open circuit potential. (e) The cycling performance of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH at the current density of 10 A/g.
(a) CP curves, (b) CV curves, and (c) cell capacitance at different current densities from 0.5 to 4 A/g. (d) cycling performance of the device at the current density of 4 A/g.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of the carbon-optimized integrated cobalt-based electrode for high cycle performance supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Chemical Engineering Institute, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300350, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering, Chemical Engineering Institute, Tianjin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Chemical Engineering Institute, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300350, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Co3O4 and nickel–cobalt layered double hydroxide (Ni-Co LDH) have been extensively studied as a typical cobalt-based pseudocapacitive materials. To further improve their electrochemical performance, the cobalt-based materials and carbon were integrated to prepare hierarchical Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays through the hydrothermal and calcination methods. According to the characterization, the results show the Co3O4 nanocones with single-crystalline structure serve as the stable frameworks and the amorphous carbon coated on the Co3O4 nanocones improves the conductivity and the structural stability significantly. Moreover, numerous active sites of Redox reactions, provided by the Ni-Co LDH, improve specific capacitance of electrode. Based on performance measurements, it was illustrated that the integrated Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes possessed the outstanding performance of specific capacitance (1755 F/g at 1 A/g) with remarkable cycling stability (specific capacitance remained at 90.47% after 5000 cycles at the current density of 10 A/g) because of the synergetic effects of high activity and conductivity Co3O4 @C scaffolds and well-defined Ni-Co LDH nanoneedles. The asymmetric supercapacitors, based on Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH // active carbon, showed an extraordinary cyclic stability with the retention rate of specific capacitance is 94.47% after 16000 cycles. Furthermore, prepared devices showed the excellent energy density with 33.02 Wh/kg with the power density was 398.85 W/kg. The excellent electrochemical properties of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes indicate that the multi-component integrated materials have great theoretical significance for the study of high-performance electrode materials, and also reflect that the Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes exhibit outstanding value and extremely promising prospect in practical application.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the large consumption of mineral fuels, electricity, as the clean and recyclable energy source, had become the substitute for the traditional energy. Compared with the traditional electrical energy storage device of battery, supercapacitors were expected to become a new generation equipment of electrical energy storage because the shorter charge/discharge time, longer life span and higher power density [1–3]. Nevertheless, the deficiency of the energy density of supercapacitors hindered the practical application of them [4,5]. Transition metal oxides/hydroxides enable fast redox reactions to provide a large theoretical specific capacitance, so many of them were studied to enhance the performance of electrodes. Among them, Ni(OH)2 [6,7], MnO2 [8], Co(OH)2 [9,10], NiO [11], V2O5 [12,13], Co3O4 [14,15], Co-Mn LDH [16], Ni-Co LDH etc. [17,18], were often used to increase the energy density of SCs. Additionally, there were some evidences that the multiple components exhibited better performance than the single component or phase by exploiting the synergistic effects [19,20]. Meanwhile, the electrode materials with unique structures exhibited enhanced working stability, which provided protective mechanism for the scaffold and hybrid components [14,21]. In addition to the common advantages of easy synthesis, Co3O4 and Ni-Co LDH were both with environment-friendliness, higher specific capacitance and redox activity, thus, they had great potential as outstanding pseudocapacitive materials of electrodes. Nevertheless, further improvement of the electrical conductivity of Ni-Co LDH and Co3O4 was necessary. Furthermore, the Ni-Co LDH was easy to agglomerate, which made the electrochemical active sites reduced on the surface and resulted in the electrochemical performance decreasing.

In order to solve the problems mentioned above, some effective measures had been taken. For examples, a layer of other pseudocapacitive material was coated on the surface of Co3O4 to increase the conductivity or protect Co3O4 from being destroyed in the reaction. And Ni-Co LDH was often loaded on other substances surface to increase its electrochemical active sites. Such as, Bai et al. synthesized one kind of hierarchical electrode material by growing nanowires of Ni-Co LDH on nickel foam coated by reduced graphene oxide (r-GO), which possessed 1041 F/g at 2.5 mA/cm2. Moreover, in the potential range between 0 with 1.6 V, the asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) delivered the electrochemical performance with 108.7 F/g with current density of 2 mA/cm2 [22]. As Kong et al. reported, MnO2 on Co3O4 nanoneedles grown on Nickel foam exhibited the capacitance of 1693.2 F/g at 1 A/g and showed excellent cyclic stability and rate capability [23]. Additionally, Hu et al. prepared two layers of Co3O4 @NiO as electrode material, which possessed outstanding cyclic stability and excellent areal capacitance with 1236.67 F/g with current density of 1 A/g [24]. Despite these achievements, further development of electrochemical properties of Ni-Co LDH and Co3O4 were still facing many obstacles in more rational structural design and a simplified route for material synthesis, which also offered advantages for the improvement of the conductivity.

It is well known that the carbon is a kind of the typical electrical double-layer electrode material. Although the pseudocapacitive materials possessed higher specific capacitance than the carbon, the carbon possesses the high conductivity and low cost. Therefore, it will be a good idea to integrate pseudocapacitive materials with the electrical double-layer electrode material to improve the electrical performance, which will make use of abundant redox sites of the former and the excellent conductivity of the later.

According to above considerations, in this paper, the hierarchical Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays with high performance was prepared on the NF by the integration of pseudocapacitive materials and double-layer capacitive materials with an economical and novel strategy. First, the Co3O4 nanowires on the nickel foam was synthesized and transformed into the Co3O4 nanocones with single-crystalline structure, serving as the stable frameworks. Then, the Co3O4 nanocones were coated with amorphous carbon formed by the carbonation of glucoses, which apparently enhanced the structural stability and the conductivity of Co3O4 in the redox reaction. Finally, Ni-Co LDH nanoneedles were deposited on the Co3O4 @C nanocones, which could provide more active sites and enhance specific surface area. By the advantages of the synergetic effects and the features of multidimensional structure, the integrated Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrode delivered high electrochemical performance, especially the cycling performance.

The synthesis process of the integrated Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays on Ni foam mainly included 3 steps. First, the Co3O4 nanowires were prepared through a simple hydrothermal process. Subsequently, Co3O4 @C nanocones were obtained by the carbonation of glucose on the Co3O4 nanowires surface. Finally, the integrated Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays were synthesized by depositing the Ni-Co LDH nanoneedles on Co3O4 @C nanocones. The schematic representation of the procedure of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays was shown in Fig. 1.

Without further purification, the chemicals and solvents were of analytical grade and could be used. First of all, immersed one piece of rectangular NF with 2 cm
×
cm in 3 M HCl solution for thirty mins to clean the surface of the NF. After that, rinsed thoroughly with ethanol and deionized water (DIW) several times. First, through a simple hydrothermal method, Co3O4 nanowires were prepared. Dissolving 0.582 g of Co(NO3)2•6 H2O, 0.148 g of NH4F and 0.6 g of CO(NH2)2 in 35 ml DIW with magnetic stirring until the solution turned to pink, then put the cleaned Ni foam (NF) into it and heated at 120 °C for 5 h in autoclave. After cooling autoclave down, collected and cleaned the sample entirely. After that, the sample was heated in air for 2 h at 350 °C and transformed into the Co3O4 nanowires. The loading mass of Co3O4 on the nickel foam was 2.50 mg/cm2. Next, the as-prepared Co3O4 nanowires were submerged in the glucose solution with the concentration of 0.04 M for 14 h, then annealed in the tube furnace at 450 °C for 2 h under atmosphere of Ar. After the furnace cooled down, the Co3O4 @C nanocones were prepared. The measured load mass of Co3O4 @C was 2.75 mg/cm2 .

Immersing Co3O4 @C nanocones in 50 ml transparent pink solution, which concluded CO(NH2)2 (4 mmol), Co(NO3)2•6 H2O (2 mmol) and Ni(NO3)2•6 H2O (1 mmol). After that, heated in the sealed Teflon autoclave for 6 h at 90 °C to deposit the Ni-Co LDH nanoneedles on Co3O4 @C nanocones. After the autoclave has cooled down, collected the sample and washed with DI water, labeled as Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays. The load mass of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH was measured to be 3.20 mg/cm2 .

The crystallinity phase and composition of the electrode materials were analyzed by the system (Rigaku Smartlab) of powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kαradiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) at 220 mA and 60 kV. For the morphologies, they were carefully characterized by performing the field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (FEI Apreo S LoVac, America) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM-F200, Japan) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The characterization of the chemical states of the elements were achieved by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on the Thermo Scientific K-Alpha at the Al Kα radiation with 12 kV at 6 mA.

Using the electrochemical workstation (CHI660E), the electrochemical tests for all the as-synthesized electrode materials were implemented with the classical system of three-electrode electrochemical cell. The as-synthesized electrode used for working electrode, the Hg/HgO electrode worked as reference electrode, the Pt foil (1 cm × 1 cm) as counter electrode, and the working electrolyte was 3 M KOH. Herein, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), galvanostatic charge−discharge (GCD) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were included in the measurements of electrochemical performance of the prepared electrode materials. The specific capacitances (
C
s
) of the as-prepared samples were calculated by using the Eq. (1) [25,26].
(1)
C
s
= ( 2 I ×
∫
Vdt
) / ( m
× ∆
V
2
)
where I (A) is the constant discharge current, t (s) is the discharging time,
∆ V
(V) is potential drop during discharge, V (V)and m (g) designate the potential and the mass of active materials on the Ni foam, respectively.

For asymmetric supercapacitor, the prepared Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH worked as positive electrode and the active carbon (AC) worked as negative electrode. On the basis of relationship of q
+
= q
- , the optimum ratio of mass of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH and AC was calculated with Eq. (2).
(2)
m
+
m
−
=
C
−
×
V
−
C
+
×
V
+
where
C
+
,
C
−
,
V
+
,
V
−
,
m
+
and
m
−
are specific capacitance (F/g), working potential range (V), and the mass (g) for positive and negative electrodes, respectively. The Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrode was directly worked as the positive electrode and active carbon (AC) as active material for negative electrode. The active material mass of the positive electrode was 1.2 mg. After calculation, the active material mass of the negative electrode was 9.0 mg. While negative electrode was synthesized as follows: First, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), acetylene black and the active carbon with the mass ratio of 1:1:8 evenly dispersed in N-methylpyrrolidinone to produce a black uniform slurry. After that, coating the obtained mixture on the nickel foam (NF) and drying the coated NF in the oven for 12 h at 60 °C. For the cell capacitance ( C ), power density ( P ) and energy density ( E ) of the supercapacitor were obtained by Eqs. (3–5) [25,26], respectively.
(3)
C
c
= ( 2 I ∫ Vdt ) / ( M
V
2
)
(4)
P =
E
∆ t
× 3600
(5)
E =
I
∫
Vdt
3.6 M
Where
∆ t
, I , V and M are the total discharging time (s), discharge current density (A/g), the potential window (V) of the supercapacitor device and total electroactive mass (g) of the cathode and the anode electrodes in the two-electrode system, respectively.

The morphologic properties of Co3O4 nanowires, Co3O4 @C nanocones and Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays were investigated using SEM technologies, shown in Fig. 2. In Fig. 2(a) and (d), it could be observed that Co3O4 nanowires were densely grown on the NF. The higher precision in Fig. 2(d) demonstrated that the Co3O4 nanowires were 2 μm in length with the diameter gradually increasing from the tip to the root. From the SEM images of Co3O4 @C nanocones in Fig. 2(b) and (e), it was observed that the Co3O4 @C nanocone was composed of several Co3O4 nanowires and showed larger diameter, which could be used as the scaffold with high structural strength for the decoration of Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays. Seeing from the image of SEM in Fig. 2(c) and (f), it revealed that the Co3O4 @C nanocones were decorated with Ni-Co LDH nanoneedles and formed the hierarchical structure, which could enlarge the specific surface area for contacting with electrolyte and provide more active sites for the Redox. Moreover, it had been reported that the hierarchical structure provided more area space than the conventional morphology of Ni-Co LDH nanosheets [22].

For further study morphology and microstructure of the as-prepared materials, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization was performed, shown in Fig. 3. It was important to note that the active materials should be scraped from the NF substrate before test for accuracy. For the damaged Co3O4 nanowires in Fig. 3(a) and 3(d), it was revealed the Co3O4 nanowires were straight and mesoporous, and the lattice spacings of 0.232 nm, 0.154 nm and 0.285 nm corresponded to the lattice plane (222), （200）and (511) of Co3O4, respectively. The TEM image of Co3O4 @C nanocones were showed in Fig. 3(b), the HRTEM image in Fig. 3(e) showed more details information about Co3O4 @C nanocones, in which the spacing plane of 0.282 nm corresponded to the (220) plane of the Co3O4 and the carbon doped on the surface of Co3O4 nanowires. And the EDS elemental mapping shown in Fig. S1 demonstrated that elements of Co, O and C were uniformly distributed. After decoration of Ni-Co LDH, it was seen from the TEM image in Fig. 3(c) that Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays were composed of Ni-Co LDH nanoneedles and Co3O4 @C nanocones. From the HRTEM image of Ni-Co LDH nanoneedles (Fig. 3f), the lattice spaces of 0.232 nm, 0.265 nm and 0.198 nm corresponded to the (015), (101) and (018) planes of Ni-Co LDH, respectively. Moreover, another HRTEM image of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays (Fig. S2) demonstrated that Ni-Co LDH nanoneedles exhibited good affinity with Co3O4 @C nanocones.

The XRD patterns of the Ni-Co LDH, Co3O4 and Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH were displayed in Fig. 4. All of the observed diffraction peaks (marked by “·”) of Ni-Co LDH were indexed to the typical α-phase Co5(O9.48H8.52)NO3 (JCPDS:4 6–0605) and the typical α-phase Ni(OH)2·0.75 H2O (JCPDS:38–0715) [27–30]. The characteristic diffraction peaks (marked by “&”) at 2θ = 19.0°, 31.3°, 36.9°, 38.5°, 44.8°, 55.7°, 59.4°, 65.2° and 77.3° were observed and coincident with the (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440) and (533) planes of the cubic Co3O4 (JCPDS card no. 42–1467), respectively. In addition, the peaks of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH centered at 42.7° and 62.0° (marked by “A”) indicated the existence of carbon in the Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH. The above results indicated that the hierarchical Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH contained cubic Co3O4, carbon and Ni-Co LDH, which were consistent with the TEM analysis results.

For further study the chemical bonding states and chemical composition of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH, the XPS tests were performed, and Fig. 5 showed the results. In Fig. 5(a), the full survey spectrum of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH showed the peaks of C 1 s, O 1 s, Ni 2p, Co 2p. Three peaks of O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 5(b) were located at 532.1 eV, 531.4 eV and 529.8 eV, corresponding to the oxygen (O2-) species in metal oxides the absorbed water (H-O-H) and the oxygen in hydroxyl bonding (-OH) respectively [31,32]. The C 1 s spectrum in Fig. 5(c) showed three distinguishable peaks at 289.1 eV, 286.4 eV and 285.0 eV, related to O-CO, C-O and C-C bonds [33–37]. The fine XPS spectrum of Co 2p in Fig. 5(d) could be divided into two shake-up satellites and two spin-orbit doublets. The spin-orbit doublets of Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 were observed at 780.7 eV and 796.3 eV, respectively, which were attributed to Co3+ [38–42]. The peaks located at 798.9 eV and 783.4 eV were belong to Co2+ [43–47]. Additionally, the two weak peaks at 787.0 eV and 803.1 eV were from the shake-up satellites and identify the co-existence of Co2+ and Co3+ [48]. For Ni element, the Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. 5e) showed a pair of peaks with higher intensity and other two weaker intensity peaks. The higher intensity peaks at 873.6 eV and 855.9 eV were related to Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2 spin orbits, respectively [49]. About the weaker peaks at 880.0 eV and 861.7 eV corresponded to shake-up satellites of Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2, respectively. The above results demonstrated the existence of the Ni2+ state [50,51]. Based on the analysis, it was proved that the successful formation of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH, which was consisted of Ni-Co LDH, carbon and Co3O4.

In order to better study the electrochemical activity of prepared Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH, the prepared electrodes were carefully studied in the three-electrode assembly system with 3 M KOH. The typical cyclic voltammograms (CVs) analysis of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes were implemented with various scan rates from 5 to 100 mV/s in the voltage window of 0–0.5 V, which was illustrated in Fig. 6(a). As demonstrated in Fig. 6(a), the shapes of the CV curves demonstrated that the electrochemical capacitance of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes were controlled by the double-layer capacitance of the doped carbon and the Faradic redox reactions of pseudocapacitive materials. Moreover, with the rates of scanning being increased, the peaks of cathode gradually moved towards the negative potential and the peaks belong to anode gradually shifted to the positive potential, which was because the redox reactions were mainly governed by the charge-transfer dynamics [52]. When the rates of scanning increased from 5 mV/s to 50 mV/s, the potential of the anodic peak shifted from 0.32 V to 0.45 V, and the CV curves kept the same shape. These results implied the Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrode exhibited outstanding rate performance, which was because the high conductivity of carbon was benefit for the electron / charge diffusion.

For further investigate the electrochemical properties of synthesized Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes, the test of galvanostatic charge-discharge（GCD） was performed under 0 − 0.5 V at different current density ranging from 1 to 16 A/g and the results were displayed in Fig. 6(b). It could be calculated that the Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes showed excellent specific capacitance of 1755 F/g at 1 A/g. Additionally, the GCD of Co3O4, Co3O4 @C and Ni-Co LDH electrodes was displayed in Fig. S3. As illustrated in Fig. S3, the Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH delivered the highest specific capacitance and even higher than the sum of the capacitance of Ni-Co LDH (137.9 F/g at 1 A/g) and Co3O4 @C (1141.4 F/g at 1 A/g) electrodes. Therefore, the following reasons could explain the high capacitance of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH. First, Co3O4 had high theoretical specific capacitance and abundant redox-active sites. Second, the excellent conductivity of carbon layer provided high-speed channels for the transportation of ions and electrons between the Ni-Co LDH and Co3O4. Then, the decorative Ni-Co LDH provided more specific area and active sites. Final, between Ni-Co LDH and Co3O4 @C existed synergistic effect.

The curve of the relationship between the current density and the specific capacitance of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes was illustrated in Fig. 6(c). Specific capacitance of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes tended to decrease gradually with the current density increasing, which resulted from the ascending voltage drops and the inadequate active sites related to redox reactions at the exorbitant current density. The assessment of electrochemical behavior of the Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes were performed through the EIS measurement. As illustrated in Fig. 6(d), the homologous Nyquist plot of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes were obtained in the frequency range from 0.01 to 100 kHz. Then, internal resistance (Rs) of the electrodes was measured by the intercept between the Nyquist plot and the X-axis intersection [53,54]. Here, the Rs of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes was calculated to be 0.584 Ω, which indicated Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrode possessed internal resistance low internal resistance. The semicircle of the plot represented charge-transfer resistance between the working electrode and electrolyte, which could indirectly characterize the conductivity of the electrode [55]. From Fig. 6(d), the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrode was estimated to be 1.12 Ω, indicating the tested electrode with high electron conductivity. The Warburg impedance represented the ion diffusivity at the electrolyte/electrode interface, and it was measured to be 2.2 Ω for Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrode. Th results suggested the Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrode exhibited excellent electrolyte ion diffusivity to satisfy the rapid Faradaic reactions.

In Fig. 6(e), the cycling capability of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrode was characterized by CP for 5000 cycles at 10 A/g within the potential window range from 0 V to 0.5 V. The capacitance of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrode retained 90.47% of its original value, showing high cycling capability. The high cyclic stability of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH benefited from the high mechanical strength of Co3O4 @C nanocones, which kept the electrode more stable during the redox and the carbon layer enhanced the corrosion resistance of Co3O4 nanocones in alkaline electrolyte. Furthermore, the core-shell structure could provide protective effect. Thus, the Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrode showed excellent cyclic stability.

To study the practical application of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH, the asymmetric supercapacitor Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH // AC was assembled with PVA-KOH as the solid electrolyte, Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH as positive electrode, as-prepared active carbon (AC) as negative electrode.

To investigate the electrochemical performance of the device of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH // AC, a series of performance measurements were implemented and Fig. 7 demonstrated the results. Fig. 7(a) showed the representative CP curves at various current densities ranging from 0.5 to 4 A/g. The IR drop could not be seen easily from CP curves, which demonstrated the asymmetric device possessed negligible internal resistance. In Fig. 7(b), the CV curves with various scan rates were shown. It was obvious that the area surrounded by the CV curve was expanded gradually as the scan rate increasing, indicating the ions in the electrolytes exhibited excellent diffusion rate [56]. Moreover, the CV curves presented the reduction peak and the oxidation peak located at about 0.4 V and 1.4 V, respectively. Redox peaks shifted slightly with the scan rate increasing, which illustrated the Faradaic electrode reactions with remarkable reversibility and fast electrode kinetics [57,58]. The device capacitances at various current densities were presented in Fig. 7(c). They were evaluated to be 73.37, 61.55, 45.31 and 24.69 F/g at 0.5, 1, 2 and 4 A/g, respectively. Also, specific capacitance of device decreased gradually with the incremental current density, which was resulted from the insufficient Faradaic reactions at high current density as well as the intrinsic resistance of the electrode [57,59]. Additionally, the device still remained 40.11% of its initial capacitance after enhanced the current density to 4 A/g (4 times), which demonstrated that the ASC device with excellent rate ability. The cyclic performance of ASC device was examined with the current density of 4 A/g for 16,000 successive GCD cycles and result was displayed in Fig. 7(d). In Fig. 7(d), the ASC device showed only 2.64% loss of the initial capacitance after 5000 cycles, 3.95% loss after 10,000 cycles, and finally remained 94.74% of the initial capacitance after nearly 16,000 cycles, which explained the great cyclic performance sufficiently. Base on some previous researches, the cyclic performance of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH // AC was better than that of some reported asymmetric devices, including MnO2/GO // HPC (94% retention after 4000 cycles) [60], MnO2/PPy // N-doped active carbon (90.6% retention after 5000 cycles) [61], NixCo1 −x LDH–ZTO//activated carbon (92.7% retention after 5000 cycles) [62], and CuCo2S4 @NiMn-LDH // activated carbon (87.6% retention after 10,000 cycles) [63].

For evaluating the power densities and the energy densities, Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH // AC was obtained. For comparison, some Co3O4- or Ni-Co LDH-based asymmetric supercapacitors were also shown in Fig. S4. For Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH // AC, when the power density enhanced from 398.85 W/kg to 2222.2 W/kg, the energy density decreased from 33.02 Wh/kg to 11.11 Wh/kg. Therefore, the result demonstrated the device exhibited better performance than many reported ASC devices [64–71].

Based on the above results, it was illustrated that the Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH not only provided novel ideas for developing novel electrode materials, but also exhibited great potential in practical energy storage applications for serving as positive electrode materials for supercapacitors.

Through the simple hydrothermal and annealing procedure, a novel hierarchical Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH nanoarrays with high performance was prepared directly on the NF by the integration of pseudocapacitive material and double-layer capacitive material through the investigations of the macro and micro morphology, analysis of phase and composition, characterizing of chemical bonding state and chemical composition of Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH using SEM, TEM, XRD and XPS, it showed that the Co3O4 nanocones with single-crystalline structure on the nickel foam served as the stable frameworks. The ultrathin layer of amorphous carbon coated on Co3O4 nanocones by the carbonation of glucoses significantly enhanced structural stability and the conductivity of Co3O4 in the redox reaction. Moreover, the capacitance of the integrated electrode was enhanced because of more efficient active sites provided by Ni-Co LDH nanoneedles. By the advantages of the synergetic effects and the features of multidimensional structure, the integrated Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes possessed the great conductivity of the double-layer capacity material of carbon and high specific capacitance contributed by Co3O4 and Ni-Co LDH, which showed great electrochemical performance. According to the performance measurements, the integrated Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH electrodes delivered an excellent specific capacitance of 1755 F/g at 1 A/g and the extraordinary cycling performance of 90.47% at 10 A/g after 5000 cycles. Additionally, the assembled HSC device Co3O4 @C@Ni-Co LDH //AC showed outstanding energy density of 33.02 Wh/kg at the power density of 398.85 W/kg. For the cyclic performance, the device displayed a remarkable cyclic stability (94.47%) after 16,000 cycles at the current density of 4 A/g. This work provided the novel idea to design and fabricate the high-performance electrodes of supercapacitors.

Zhixiang Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft preparation, Experiment. Yan wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Financial support. Wang fengcai: Supervision, Financial support, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.164158.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hierarchical CuCo2S4@NiMn-layered double hydroxide core-shell hybrid arrays as electrodes for supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Lin, H. Jia, H. Liang, S. Chen, Y. Cai, J. Qi, C. Qu, J. Cao, W. Fei, J. Feng, Hierarchical CuCo2S4@NiMn-layered double hydroxide core-shell hybrid arrays as electrodes for supercapacitors, Chemical Engineering Journal, 336 (2018) 562–569.

Han, Shish-kebab type MnCo2O4@Co3O4 nanoneedle arrays derived from MnCo-LDH@ZIF-67 for high-performance supercapacitors and efficient oxygen evolution reaction, Chemical Engineering Journal, 354 (2018) 875–884.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Wang, Y. Li, S. Li, X. Liao, S.
